topic: A person learns their college is closing down
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. Subject line: Important Notice Regarding Academic Programs. My fingers hovered over the screen, the cheap plastic of my mouse feeling suddenly slick and foreign under my touch. I clicked. The words swam into view, a cascade of bureaucratic jargon culminating in a stark, undeniable statement: Maplewood University will cease operations at the end of the fiscal year. My breath hitched. I reread the paragraph about faculty transitions, my eyes darting frantically across the page, searching for a loophole, a typo, anything that suggested this was a misunderstanding. A cold knot tightened in my stomach, spreading outward until my hands began to tremble uncontrollably. I stood up, pacing the small dorm room floor, the sound of my worn sneakers echoing too loudly in the sudden silence. I needed to call someone, anyone, but my throat constricted, refusing to form a coherent sentence.

[story 2]

I found myself staring blankly at the campus map, the vibrant green of the quad suddenly seeming mocking in its permanence. Every familiar landmark—the ivy-covered library, the bustling student union—felt weighted with impending absence. When the official announcement finally hit me via a campus-wide broadcast, I physically recoiled, pressing my palms against my ears as if to block out the sound of my own frantic heartbeat. *No. This isn't real.* I started checking my phone incessantly, refreshing the news sites, my thumb moving with a desperate, jerky rhythm. Sweat prickled my hairline. I walked to the window, peering out at the familiar skyline, but everything looked muted, as if the world itself was holding its breath. A deep, unsettling hollowness settled in my chest, a profound sense of disorientation that made my knees weak. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the image of the closing doors away.

[story 3]

The administrative office smelled faintly of old paper and stale coffee, a scent that usually brought a sense of comfortable routine. Today, it just made my nausea worse. The dean sat across the desk, his expression carefully neutral, but I could see the slight tension around his jaw. When he delivered the news—the complete shuttering of the institution—my carefully constructed composure shattered. I felt a sudden, sharp pressure behind my eyes, a dizzying sensation that made the fluorescent lights above flicker. I gripped the edge of the desk so tightly my knuckles turned white. "Wait," I managed to croak, the sound thin and reedy. "Is this... is this temporary? Can we appeal this decision?" My voice cracked on the last word. I felt a rush of hot, panicked energy flooding my system, compelling me to demand answers, to fight against the undeniable reality settling around me.
